figures.caption: Figure 3: Visualization of the dynamic merging process. Red bounding boxes indicate the specific Transformer blocks where the RL agent executes token merging, with the corresponding block index annotated in the top-right corner. Unmarked images represent blocks where the agent opted for zero-merging actions (bypassing the reduction step).

Table 1: Quantitative comparison of token reduction methods on ImageNet-1K. We align the Top-1 accuracy of all methods to ensure a fair comparison of computational efficiency. The highest token reduction rate and lowest GFLOPS under identical accuracy constraints are highlighted in bold.

Figure 2: Computational savings comparison of token reduction methods under strictly aligned Top-1 accuracy constraints. (a) Normalized FLOPs results on the ViT architectures. (b) Normalized FLOPs results on the DeiT architectures.

Table 3: Comprehensive robustness evaluation on Out-of-Distribution (OOD) datasets. We evaluate the methods across natural adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), renditions (ImageNet-R), and three macro-categories of corruptions (ImageNet-C). Under equivalent token reduction rates, DORA consistently maintains higher accuracy. When constrained to match DORA’s accuracy, the heuristic baseline (ToMe[4]) suffers severe degradation in its token reduction capabilities.

Figure 1: Overall architecture of the proposed DORA framework. The framework employs a decoupled design, consisting of an offline training phase and an online inference phase. During the online phase, a pre-trained lightweight Actor network dynamically generates token merging masks, enabling input-adaptive acceleration for Transformers. In the offline phase, a high-capacity Critic network assists in training and optimizing the Actor’s policy via the PPO algorithm [13] and a reward based on knowledge distillation. The Critic is discarded after training.
